summary: Deuteronomy 32:8 exhibits one of the most significant theologically motivated textual revisions in the Hebrew Bible, where MT's 'sons of Israel' (בני ישראל) replaces an earlier reading 'sons of God/gods' (בני אלהים/אלוהים) preserved in LXX, 4QDeutj, and 4QDeutq. This revision eliminates a polytheistic cosmology wherein Elyon apportions nations to divine beings, replacing it with a monotheistic framework centered on Israel's election. The change reflects Second Temple period concerns to suppress references to a divine council and subordinate deities.
summary_plain: The Masoretic Text changed 'sons of God' to 'sons of Israel' in this verse, removing an earlier idea that God divided the nations among divine beings. This reflects later Jewish concerns to eliminate any hint of multiple gods and emphasize Israel's special relationship with the one God. The Dead Sea Scrolls and Greek translation preserve the original reading.

revisions.mt_reading: When the Most High gave to the nations their inheritance, when he separated the sons of mankind, he fixed the borders of the peoples according to the number of the sons of Israel (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל)
revisions.revised_reading: Original reading preserved in LXX (υἱῶν θεοῦ 'sons of God'), 4QDeutj (בני אלהים 'sons of God'), and 4QDeutq (בני אלוהים 'sons of gods'): 'according to the number of the sons of God'
revisions.evidence: The MT reading creates a chronological impossibility: nations are divided 'according to the number of sons of Israel' before Israel exists as a nation. The Qumran manuscripts (4QDeutj, 4QDeutq) from the 2nd-1st century BCE preserve בני אלהים, confirming this as the original Hebrew reading. LXX's υἱῶν θεοῦ independently attests the pre-MT tradition. The revision eliminates explicit reference to a divine council (cf. Ps 82:1, 6; 89:6-7; Job 1:6) where Elyon/Yahweh apportions nations to lesser deities, a cosmology increasingly problematic for emerging monotheism. The change to 'sons of Israel' reinterprets the verse within Israel-centered salvation history rather than polytheistic world-ordering.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Masoretic reading doesn't make sense chronologically—how could nations be divided according to the number of Israelites before Israel existed? Ancient manuscripts from the Dead Sea Scrolls show the original said 'sons of God,' referring to divine beings in God's heavenly court who were assigned to rule the nations.
scholarly_citations: Skehan, 'A Fragment of the Song of Moses (Deut 32) from Qumran', BASOR 136 (1954), pp. 12-15
Heiser, 'Deuteronomy 32:8 and the Sons of God', Bibliotheca Sacra 158 (2001), pp. 52-74
van der Kooij, 'The Ending of the Song of Moses: On the Pre-Masoretic Version of Deut 32:43', in Studies in Deuteronomy (1994), pp. 93-100
Tigay, Deuteronomy: The JPS Torah Commentary (1996), pp. 302-303, 514
Tov, Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible (2012), pp. 269-270
revisions.counter_arguments: Some argue 'sons of Israel' could be original if understood as prophetic anticipation or if the seventy nations tradition (Gen 10) was coordinated with Jacob's seventy descendants (Gen 46:27, Exod 1:5). However, this requires forced harmonization and ignores the manuscript evidence.

revisions.mt_reading: he fixed the borders of the peoples according to the number of the sons of Israel (MT)
revisions.revised_reading: Targum Onqelos: 'according to the number of the seventy souls of the sons of Israel' (למניין שבעין נפשן דבני ישראל); Targum Neofiti and Pseudo-Jonathan elaborate similarly, connecting to the seventy nations and seventy Israelites
revisions.evidence: The Targums preserve the MT's 'sons of Israel' but expand it to explicitly reference the seventy souls who went down to Egypt (Gen 46:27, Exod 1:5), thereby harmonizing Deut 32:8 with the tradition of seventy nations in Genesis 10 (counted as seventy in rabbinic tradition). This proto-rabbinic exegesis resolves the chronological tension by creating a numerical correspondence: seventy nations matched to seventy Israelites, suggesting predestination of Israel's role among the nations. The expansion is clearly interpretive rather than translational, indicating theological concern to domesticate the verse within pentateuchal narrative consistency.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translations add the detail about 'seventy souls' to connect this verse with the story of Jacob's family going to Egypt and the tradition that there were seventy nations in the world. This makes the verse fit better with other parts of the Torah.
scholarly_citations: Grossfeld, The Targum Onqelos to Deuteronomy (1988), p. 90
revisions.counter_arguments: The Targumic expansion could be seen as legitimate midrashic clarification of an ambiguous phrase rather than theological revision, though the specificity suggests motivated interpretation.

revisions.mt_reading: according to the number of the sons of Israel (MT)
revisions.revised_reading: Peshitta: 'according to the number of the sons of the angels' (ܠܡܢܝܢܐ ܕܒܢܝ̈ ܡܠܐܟ̈ܐ, lmenyānā d-bnay mal'ākē)
revisions.evidence: The Peshitta represents an intermediate tradition, translating 'sons of angels' rather than 'sons of God' or 'sons of Israel.' This reflects a demythologizing strategy: acknowledging the original divine council cosmology while reinterpreting the בני אלהים as angels (created beings) rather than divine beings proper, thus mitigating polytheistic implications. This interpretive move parallels LXX Job 1:6; 2:1 where בני האלהים is rendered ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ. The Peshitta's reading likely depends on a Hebrew Vorlage reading בני אלהים but interprets it through an angelological lens consistent with Second Temple Jewish and early Christian cosmology, where the divine council is reimagined as an angelic hierarchy.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Syriac translation uses 'sons of angels' instead of either 'sons of God' or 'sons of Israel.' This is a compromise: it acknowledges that divine beings were involved in dividing the nations, but calls them angels (created servants) rather than gods, making the passage less threatening to monotheism.
scholarly_citations: Weitzman, The Syriac Version of the Old Testament (1999), pp. 82-84
Dirksen, 'The Old Testament Peshitta', in Mikra (1988), p. 272
revisions.counter_arguments: The Peshitta's 'angels' reading might reflect early Christian interpretive traditions (cf. LXX Dan 10:13, 20-21) rather than direct theological revision of a Hebrew text, making it derivative rather than independent witness to theological concern.

revisions.mt_reading: according to the number of the sons of Israel (MT)
revisions.revised_reading: Vulgate: 'juxta numerum filiorum Israhel' ('according to the number of the sons of Israel'), following MT
revisions.evidence: Jerome's Vulgate follows the MT reading 'sons of Israel,' likely because he translated from the Hebrew text available in Palestine in the late 4th century CE, which already contained the revised reading. However, Jerome was aware of the LXX variant 'sons of God' (αγγέλων θεοῦ in some LXX manuscripts) and discusses textual variants in his commentaries. His choice to follow the MT may reflect both the Hebrew text at his disposal and theological preference for a reading that avoided explicit divine council language. The Vulgate thus transmits the post-exilic Jewish theological revision into Christian Latin tradition, where it became authoritative for Western Christianity until modern critical editions.
revisions.evidence_plain: Jerome's Latin translation follows the 'sons of Israel' reading found in the Hebrew texts of his day (around 400 CE). He knew about the 'sons of God' reading in Greek manuscripts but chose to follow the Hebrew, passing the revised Jewish reading into Western Christian Bibles.
scholarly_citations: Brown, 'Jerome and the Vulgate', in The New Cambridge History of the Bible (2012), pp. 355-356
revisions.counter_arguments: Jerome's adherence to the Hebrew text reflects his stated principle of 'Hebraica veritas' (Hebrew truth) rather than necessarily theological agreement with the revision; he may have been unaware of the textual-critical issues or deemed the Hebrew authoritative regardless of content.

dominant_strategy: Softening is the dominant revision strategy in Deuteronomy 32:8, specifically the suppression of explicit polytheistic/henotheistic cosmology. The MT's replacement of 'sons of God' with 'sons of Israel' represents a major theological redaction eliminating reference to a divine council where subordinate deities receive territorial assignments. This reflects late Second Temple Jewish monotheistic concerns to eradicate vestiges of Israelite polytheism that acknowledged Yahweh's supremacy within a pantheon (cf. Ps 82, 89:5-8). Subsequent traditions either preserve this revision (Vulgate), further domesticate it through harmonization (Targums), or attempt compromise formulations (Peshitta's angels).
overall_assessment: The textual history of Deuteronomy 32:8 reveals a deliberate Second Temple period theological revision wherein scribes replaced an original reference to 'sons of God' (divine beings in Elyon's council) with 'sons of Israel,' fundamentally reorienting the verse from polytheistic cosmology to Israel-centered election theology. The Qumran evidence (4QDeutj, 4QDeutq) demonstrates this revision post-dates the 2nd century BCE, while LXX's independent preservation of the original reading confirms widespread knowledge of the pre-Masoretic tradition. The tradents responsible for the MT revision participated in a broader program of monotheistic redaction visible throughout the Hebrew Bible, systematically eliminating or reinterpreting references to the divine council (cf. revisions in Psalms, Job). Subsequent versions either transmitted this revision uncritically (Vulgate) or employed further harmonizing strategies (Targums' seventy souls, Peshitta's angelology) to domesticate the verse's mythological resonances within acceptable theological frameworks.
overall_plain: This verse originally described God dividing the world's nations among divine beings in his heavenly council, but Jewish scribes changed it to refer to 'sons of Israel' instead, removing the polytheistic implications. The Dead Sea Scrolls prove this change happened relatively late (sometime after 200 BCE). Later translations either kept this revision or tried to explain it in ways that wouldn't threaten belief in one God, such as calling the divine beings 'angels' or connecting the verse to other biblical stories about the number seventy.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Divine Council Erased: MT's Monotheizing Revision Strategy
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The MT revision of Deuteronomy 32:8 represents a watershed moment in biblical theology where Second Temple Jewish scribes systematically suppressed vestiges of early Israelite polytheism/henotheism. The replacement of 'sons of God' with 'sons of Israel' is not merely textual corruption but deliberate theological censorship, transforming a cosmological myth of divine territorial apportionment into an Israel-centric salvation-historical framework. This reveals that the canonical Hebrew text underwent significant ideological redaction well into the Hellenistic period, challenging traditional notions of textual stability and demonstrating that 'biblical theology' as preserved in MT often reflects late Second Temple concerns rather than earlier Israelite religion.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: The change from 'sons of God' to 'sons of Israel' shows that the Hebrew Bible we have today was deliberately edited to remove ideas about multiple gods, even though these ideas were originally part of ancient Israelite religion. This editing happened surprisingly late—just a few centuries before Jesus.
